fr:title.text: Forester 5.0 [TBA] › new datalog-based query engine › an example datalog query

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Forester 5.0 [TBA] › new datalog-based query engine › an example datalog query

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: In order to illustrate the use of the datalog engine, I will show how to construct a query that will find all the trees with taxon reference written by jonmsterling that are tagged either accepted or refereed. The first thing we must do is define the smallest relation containing all trees tagged accepted or refereed; this is done by adding clauses to the database as follows.
First we define the name of a relation. In the current release, relations are global strings and there is no facility to generate a “fresh” one; that may change in the future.

Next, we add two defining clauses for our relation. In what follows, \datalog simply delimits content that should be parsed as a datalog expression, and \execute instructs Forester to execute the datalog expression.

The left-hand side of the reversed turnstile -: is a proposition representing the goal; the right hand side is given by propositions representing the premises, each surrounded in braces and separated by whitespace. Metavariables are written with a question mark sigil. Literal content is embedded into a datalog proposition by surrounding it in '{...}; we will see that literal content is one kind of vertex in the graph database.
Now that we have actually defined the relation that we will use, we can run a query:

The structure of a query identifies the metavariable being solved behind the turnstile, and then lists the clauses that must hold. Whereas a content vertex is addressed via '{...}, a URL vertex is addressed using @{...}.

As the query engine has evolved, it has become a bit unwieldy and misshapen. In order to prevent further churn and facilitate the long-term development and use of Forester, we are replacing the query engine and graph analysis with a more general tool: datalog. With this change comes the ability to introduce arbitrary relations of any arity; this is a powerful and dangerous tool, so use it wisely.

Switching to datalog makes forests that make heavy use of queries a bit slower to render. We considered this trade-off carefully, but believe that for the long-term health of Forester, this is acceptable. In the near future, Forester will render trees on demand rather than all at once (except when you are preparing a static archive for upload/deployment); in this mode of use, Forester will be faster than ever.

Forester’s built-in binary relations have been accessible via macros like \rel/authors, etc. In this release, these relations are being renamed to make them more pleasant and consistent to use with datalog.
A few new built-in relations have been added.

fr:ul.fr:li: \rel/is-node ?X is a unary relation that holds for any tree address ?X. This is needed when specifying reflexive binary relations: the clause R ?X ?X -: is not allowed by datalog, so one must use R ?X ?X -: {\rel/is-node X}.
\rel/transcludes/reflexive-transitive-closure is the reflexive transitive closure of \rel/transcludes.

\rel/is-reference ?X holds when ?X is designated as a reference/bibliographical tree. Currently, the built-in rules of the database deduce \rel/is-reference ?X for any tree ?X with taxon reference. The purpose of abstracting from the taxon is to enable non-English users of Forester to benefit from the computation of bibliographies.
Similarly, \rel/is-person ?X holds when ?X is designated as a personal/biographical tree. Currently, the built-in rules of the database deduce \rel/is-reference ?X for any tree ?X with taxon person.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: All of these built-in relations can be defined in Forester’s dialect of datalog; they are predefined for convenience, and because they are used in the computation of backmatter.

In order to illustrate the use of the datalog engine, I will show how to construct a query that will find all the trees with taxon reference written by jonmsterling that are tagged either accepted or refereed. The first thing we must do is define the smallest relation containing all trees tagged accepted or refereed; this is done by adding clauses to the database as follows.
First we define the name of a relation. In the current release, relations are global strings and there is no facility to generate a “fresh” one; that may change in the future.

Next, we add two defining clauses for our relation. In what follows, \datalog simply delimits content that should be parsed as a datalog expression, and \execute instructs Forester to execute the datalog expression.

The left-hand side of the reversed turnstile -: is a proposition representing the goal; the right hand side is given by propositions representing the premises, each surrounded in braces and separated by whitespace. Metavariables are written with a question mark sigil. Literal content is embedded into a datalog proposition by surrounding it in '{...}; we will see that literal content is one kind of vertex in the graph database.
Now that we have actually defined the relation that we will use, we can run a query:

The structure of a query identifies the metavariable being solved behind the turnstile, and then lists the clauses that must hold. Whereas a content vertex is addressed via '{...}, a URL vertex is addressed using @{...}.

fr:p.html:mark: The old query engine is deprecated as of this release and will be removed in a subsequent release.

fr:title.text: Forester 5.0 [TBA] › a URL scheme for tree addresses

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Forester 5.0 [TBA] › a URL scheme for tree addresses

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Up until this release, Forester’s trees were addressed by short strings — which, by convention, might be of the form xxx-NNNN or some arbitrary string like sterling-2021-thesis. In order to prepare for the future in which multiple forests can be glued together, we replace Forester’s addresses with a URL scheme that incorporates a host that demarcates the current forest. A typical tree will then have an address like this:
The host is configured in your forest.toml file; for example:

When evaluating trees, addresses are always resolved against the base URL forest://host/. Therefore, a link to address xxx-NNNN in my own forest will be resolved to forest://jonmsterling/xxx-NNNN. Of course, you can also write fully resolved links (including to other forests, or to resources addressed under any other protocol). It follows that existing internal links will continue to work fine without modification; for external links, the new implementation includes some syntactic validation, so it is possible that one or two links may need to be repaired.

Today, “URLs” are a mostly implementation-defined concept that can only be understood by looking at the source code of major web browsers; in the future, this may congeal into an actual standard but today is not that day (nor is tomorrow, if the state of the whatwg/url “living” [non-]standard is any indication). In the meanwhile, Forester is implementing URLs in terms of Internationalized Resource Identifiers (RFC 3987); this standard improves on URIs by supporting unicode identifiers, which we need at the outset. It is best to think of Forester’s adoption of RFC 3987 as provisional and subject to evolution as the Web continues to mature.
